nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs and associated reef-flat habitats in the western Atlantic. Generally found in association with sea anemones — most commonly Condylactis gigantea (Atlantic giant anemone) and Bartholomea annulata (corkscrew anemone), but also reported with Lebrunia spp., Stichodactyla helianthus, and the upside-down jellyfish Cassiopea xamachana.

decorPreferences: established live rock with crevices and overhangs
a host anemone (Condylactis gigantea preferred; Bartholomea annulata, Lebrunia spp. also accepted)
low to moderate flow — strong currents are reported to stress this species

diet.notes: In the wild, functions partly as a cleaner — picking parasites, dead tissue, and mucus from passing reef fish — and partly as a scavenger feeding on detritus and small invertebrates around its host anemone. In captivity, generally accepts a wide range of prepared foods including frozen mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, pellets, and flakes. Has been reported to take food 2-3 times per week and benefits from small, varied portions rather than infrequent large feedings.

goodWith: host anemones (Condylactis gigantea, Bartholomea annulata, Lebrunia spp.)
peaceful reef fish that may visit for cleaning
other peaceful cleaner shrimp (Lysmata spp.) when ample space is available
avoidWith: arrow crabs (Stenorhynchus seticornis) — known shrimp predators
coral banded shrimp (Stenopus hispidus) — territorial toward other shrimp
clownfish — may harass anemone-associated shrimp on the host
larger wrasses, hawkfish, lionfish, triggerfish and other shrimp-eating fish

imageCandidates.notes: Laszlo Ilyes; 2912x4368. Tack-sharp in-situ shot on Condylactis anemone tentacles showing the transparent body with orange-and-white saddle spots and purple-banded legs/claws - the exact host-anemone context hobbyists see.

Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 720x960. Very vibrant three-quarter view with a red sponge backdrop; clearly shows red-banded chelae, purple-white banded legs and pink saddle markings. Light corner signature watermark.
Betty Wills; 1024x761. Sharp frontal view on a green anemone host, showing the spotted carapace and bold purple-banded legs from a different angle for variety.

summary: The Spotted Anemone Shrimp (Periclimenes yucatanicus) is a small, brightly patterned Caribbean cleaner shrimp that lives commensally with sea anemones — most often the Atlantic giant anemone (Condylactis gigantea). It is generally considered reef-safe and peaceful.
careNotes: The Spotted Anemone Shrimp is the western Atlantic and Caribbean counterpart of the Indo-Pacific Periclimenes brevicarpalis (known in the trade as the White Spot Anemone Shrimp or Pacific Clown Anemone Shrimp). The two species are commonly confused; some retailer listings apply the Indo-Pacific common names to P. yucatanicus. The Caribbean species is distinguished by a transparent body with brown-and-white saddle-shaped markings and red, purple, and white banded chelae (claws) and legs, and it associates with Atlantic anemone hosts — Condylactis gigantea, Bartholomea annulata, Lebrunia spp., and Stichodactyla helianthus — rather than the Indo-Pacific carpet anemones typically associated with P. brevicarpalis. The Spotted Anemone Shrimp is generally considered relatively uncommon in the aquarium trade compared with its Pacific counterpart, and availability tends to be inconsistent even through specialty marine suppliers. Recommended salinity runs from SG 1.022 to 1.025. Minimum tank-size guidance in the literature ranges from 40 L to 75 L; a tank of at least 75 L gives a single shrimp or small group the stable water parameters and adequate territory around its host anemone that this species tends to need. Like all crustaceans, the Spotted Anemone Shrimp is sensitive to copper-based medications, so a tank that has previously been treated with copper is not suitable for this species. Magnesium and iodine are commonly reported to support healthy molting; weekly trace-element dosing or routine reef-quality water changes generally cover this. Flow should be low to moderate — fast-moving water is reported to stress this species. Captive lifespans of around 1 to 2 years have been reported, with laboratory data suggesting a median of roughly 260 days and a maximum of around 728 days. No coral nipping has been documented. Escape risk is low to moderate — shrimp have been known to crawl out of open-top tanks, so a covered or rimmed setup is generally advised.
breedingNotes: The Spotted Anemone Shrimp has been reported to breed in captivity but is generally considered very difficult to rear. Females carry 100 to 200 small eggs tucked under the pleopods (the small paddle-like appendages on the underside of the abdomen); gravid females are commonly observed during summer in wild populations. Successful captive rearing is generally understood to require a dedicated larval tank with stable parameters, gentle flow, and very fine food such as rotifers or microalgae. Adults are reported to reach reproductive maturity at roughly 3.5 mm carapace length.

primary.name: Aquarium Breeder — Periclimenes yucatanicus Detailed Guide
primary.notes: Dedicated species guide covering water parameters, diet, host anemones, tank size, and breeding. Used as primary per controller assignment.

additional.name: Reeflings — Spotted Cleaner Shrimp (Periclimenes yucatanicus) Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Used as secondary because LiveAquaria (assigned secondary) does not carry P. yucatanicus — their 'White Spot Anemone Shrimp' page covers the Indo-Pacific P. brevicarpalis (sw-crus-008) instead. Cross-checked temperature (24-28°C), salinity (SG 1.023-1.025), and care level (moderate-to-easy). Adult-size figure (5 cm) treated as an outlier vs. Aquarium Breeder and Wikipedia (both ~2.5 cm).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Spotted Cleaner Shrimp
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Palaemonidae / Decapoda / Caridea), Caribbean range confirmation, depth (down to ~24 m), host-anemone list, and the planktonic-larval-stage detail that disagrees with Aquarium Breeder's direct-development claim.

additional.name: Quality Marine — Spotted Cleaner Shrimp product page
additional.notes: Used cautiously — the page confuses P. yucatanicus with P. brevicarpalis (calling it 'White Spot, Pacific Clown' and 'Indo-Pacific'). Only the generic reef water-parameter targets (pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12) and reef-safe rating were taken from it; species-specific claims were ignored in favor of the other sources.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Sources do not give a typical molt interval. Magnesium and iodine are commonly cited as important for healthy molting; weekly trace-element dosing or routine reef-grade water changes generally cover this. Old exoskeletons are normally left in the tank for calcium reuptake, and hiding places (live rock crevices, the host anemone's tentacle column) are reported to be critical during the vulnerable post-molt window.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed in captivity. Females carry 100-200 eggs; larvae are generally understood to be planktonic and require very fine food and stable parameters in a dedicated larval tank to survive.
